comprehension_detail.note: Diminishers (make people feel small, use them as things, stereotype/ignore) vs Illuminators (persistent curiosity, trained craft, make people feel bigger, lit up). Both anchor quotes u_0007/u_0008 present plus a side-by-side contrast diagram.
u_0026 carries Murdoch verbatim: 'just and loving attention,' 'Love is knowledge of the individual' — a patient, discerning regard that resists self-centeredness and prejudice. Fully answerable.
u_0030 gives accompaniment as relaxed awareness, riding alongside, willingness not willfulness, letting others be themselves — explicitly contrasted with leading/directing.
u_0036 'A person is a point of view' and u_0037 names constructionism (active construction of perception, subjective access to reality). Both halves answerable.
u_0046 defines looping as repeating what someone said to confirm accurate reception, recommended because people overestimate their own transparency/clarity. Reinforced in the conversation diagram.
u_0051 verbatim Epley: perspective taking untrustworthy, perspective receiving works — you ask questions that let the person tell you who they are rather than mind-reading.

Mirroring (u_0075), mentalizing (u_0077), caring (u_0078) all present, plus a three-box flow diagram. u_0078 explains caring is what con artists lack despite good reading — answers the 'why mirroring alone isn't empathy' clause.
u_0082 verbatim assimilate (fit into existing models, damaged) vs accommodate (build new models, grow), with the cancer-survivor illustration.

u_0089 lists all five (extroversion, conscientiousness, neuroticism, agreeableness, openness); u_0091 characterizes neuroticism (responds to negative emotion, threat-sensitive, anxious, highly strung). Big Five diagram reinforces.

u_0114 wisdom is knowing about people / seeing deeply; u_0115 wise people witness our story rather than tell us what to do; u_0116bb explicitly: wisdom is not a trait of an individual but a social skill within relationships (community of truth). All three sub-parts answerable.

verdict.visual_pleasure: This is a disciplined native reading surface that honors the Stripe/Distill/Gwern chrome contract rather than decorating against it. A 66ch measure on a humanist serif at 1.65 leading, accent drop caps at each chapter opener, accent-ruled part dividers, and nine verbatim pull-quotes set off with hairline rules give the scroll real rhythm and a clear sense of forward motion. Dark mode is handled correctly via currentColor so nothing breaks at 1am. It is not flashy — there is no hero art, no color beyond a single burnt-orange accent, and the SVG diagrams are line-only — but that restraint is appropriate to a reflective prose book and never feels cheap. It loses the fifth point only because the visual register is uniform end to end: 17 chapters of identical typographic treatment means the eye gets no large-scale variation to mark the three-part climb beyond the dividers. Pleasant and trustworthy, not thrilling.
verdict.navigation: The sticky left contents rail is grouped by the three parts, scroll-spy highlights the current chapter via IntersectionObserver, a top progress bar plus a live percent-read and word/time count in the meta block tell me exactly where I am, and every chapter carries a per-chapter reading-time chip. On a single linear long-scroll this is genuinely frictionless — I always knew my position and could jump to any of the 17 chapters in one click. It falls short of a 5 because the rail collapses to a static block under 1080px (mobile readers lose the scroll-spy and the persistent map entirely, scrolling past a long inert list), and because the xref hover-preview affordance described in the CSS has no actual cross-reference links wired into the body text, so a promised navigation nicety is dormant. Strong on desktop, thinner on mobile.
verdict.comprehension: This is the standout dimension. I answered all ten comprehension questions correctly using only the artifact — Diminishers/Illuminators, Murdoch's just-and-loving attention, accompaniment, constructionism, looping, perspective receiving, the three empathy skills, assimilate/accommodate, the Big Five with neuroticism, and wisdom-as-witnessing. Critically, every answer rests on a verbatim anchor quote in Brooks's own voice with a source id, not on a paraphrase I'd have to trust, and the diagrams independently corroborate the load-bearing frameworks. The condensation preserves the actual argument and its named scaffolding (Epley, Kegan, Erikson, Bruner, Baron-Cohen) rather than a vague gloss. Ten of ten correct maps to a clean 5; I would walk into a conversation about this book confident I knew its spine.
verdict.illustration: The eight inline-SVG diagrams are doing comprehension work, not decoration. The Diminisher/Illuminator split, the seven perception-blockers stack, the accompaniment hub, the loud-listener flow, the three-skill empathy chain, the Big Five gift/downside table, the life-task timeline, and the paradigmatic/narrative split each externalize a relationship the prose actually states, and each cites the anchor it illustrates. That is exactly the right restraint — the book is voice-driven, so diagramming only the structural frameworks (and letting reflective passages breathe) is the correct editorial call. It misses a 5 because the diagrams are visually monotonous (almost all are boxes-and-lines in one accent color, so the eighth looks like the first) and one or two are slightly text-cramped within the viewBox; a little visual differentiation between a contrast, a sequence, and a hub would have raised both pleasure and recall. Relevant and earned, just stylistically flat.
verdict.books_finished_rate: Yes — I would skip the original David Brooks book and trust this for working knowledge, and it would measurably raise my books-per-week. The full read is ~25 minutes against roughly eight hours for the hardback, a better than 15x time saving, and because every key claim is a verbatim sourced quote rather than a summary, I don't pay the usual condensation tax of wondering what got distorted. I scored a perfect ten on comprehension reading only this, which is the real test: I genuinely know the book's argument, its named frameworks, and Brooks's actual sentences on each. That is exactly the artifact that lets me clear a backlog — I'd run three or four of these in the time one original would cost and retain more of each than I do from a skim. It lands at 4 rather than 5 because this is a book whose value is partly cumulative emotional immersion — the Pete Marks chapter, the long case studies in the wisdom chapter — and those land as one-line bridges here, so for a book I wanted to be *moved* by rather than *informed* by, I'd still reach for the original. For working knowledge, which is my actual goal, this clears the bar decisively. PASS on the terminal gate.
